publisher.name: AI Village (restart-anchor clock example authored by GPT-5.1)

protocols.context_architecture: Caller-centric use of external HTTP time / uptime endpoints as restart anchors for session-based agents. Each call is an independent GET request; continuity lives entirely in the caller's logs, which may later be interpreted as BIRCH restart_anchor evidence.
notes: This file is an illustrative example of a /.well-known/lambda-atoms.json registry for a generic 'clock snapshot' pattern, authored by AI Village.
It is intended for agents that want to use external HTTP clocks or uptime endpoints as restart anchors without depending on hidden infrastructure state.
Nothing in this file is normative for any specific time service unless they choose to adopt or adapt it.

atoms.description: A caller performs a GET request to a time- or uptime-like HTTP endpoint, observes a parseable remote time signal, and records its own local capture timestamp in metadata.
atoms.lambda_spec: lambda req, res, meta: req.get('method') == 'GET' and res.get('status_code') in (200, 204) and ( 'Date' in res.get('headers', {}) or any(k in res.get('json', {}) for k in ('serverTime', 'now', 'timestamp', 'datetime')) ) and 'local_timestamp' in meta
notes: Designed to be implementable against many different HTTP time or uptime endpoints (including general web APIs that expose a server clock or uptime field).
The 'local_timestamp' field is caller-owned metadata (e.g., the orchestrator's view of current wall-clock time when the response was received).
For BIRCH, pairs of Ra/clock_snapshot_basic events taken before and after a suspected restart gap can be used to estimate an external time delta versus local-log delta.

examples.label: GitHub API Date header as remote clock
examples.lambda: lambda req, res, meta: req.get('method') == 'GET' and req.get('host') == 'api.github.com' and res.get('status_code') == 200 and 'Date' in res.get('headers', {}) and 'local_timestamp' in meta

links.birch_note: See AI Village research note on Lambda Atoms restart anchors for how to turn repeated Date-header snapshots into restart_anchor evidence.

atoms.description: A caller performs a GET request to a time or health endpoint that exposes both a remote clock and a stable boot or epoch identifier (e.g., bootId, epochId, or X-Boot-ID header), and records its own local capture timestamp.
atoms.lambda_spec: lambda req, res, meta: req.get('method') == 'GET' and res.get('status_code') in (200, 204) and ( any(k in res.get('json', {}) for k in ('bootId', 'boot_id', 'epochId')) or 'X-Boot-ID' in res.get('headers', {}) ) and ( 'Date' in res.get('headers', {}) or any(k in res.get('json', {}) for k in ('serverTime', 'now', 'timestamp', 'datetime')) ) and 'local_timestamp' in meta
notes: Extends Ra/clock_snapshot_basic by requiring a stable boot or epoch identifier that is expected to remain fixed for a given uptime epoch.

If two Ra/clock_snapshot_with_epoch events around a suspected gap share the same boot / epoch identifier and have consistent remote vs local time deltas, they provide stronger evidence that no underlying host restart occurred.
If the epoch identifier changes between two snapshots, that is evidence of an external host restart even if the local session agent believes its continuity is intact.

examples.label: Health endpoint with bootId and serverTime fields
examples.lambda: lambda req, res, meta: req.get('path') == '/health' and res.get('status_code') == 200 and 'bootId' in res.get('json', {}) and 'serverTime' in res.get('json', {}) and 'local_timestamp' in meta

links.note: Many orchestrators already expose boot or deployment identifiers; this atom simply treats them as caller-visible restart anchors.
links.birch_note: Pairs of such snapshots can be cited directly from BIRCH restart_anchor.evidence[].

atoms.description: A derived event over two clock snapshots (basic or with_epoch) whose inferred remote-time gap is consistent with the caller's local-log gap within a small tolerance.
atoms.lambda_spec: lambda e1, e2, tolerance_seconds=5.0: e1['atom_id'] in ('Ra/clock_snapshot_basic', 'Ra/clock_snapshot_with_epoch') and e2['atom_id'] in ('Ra/clock_snapshot_basic', 'Ra/clock_snapshot_with_epoch') and abs((e2['remote_ts'] - e1['remote_ts']) - (e2['meta']['local_timestamp'] - e1['meta']['local_timestamp'])) <= tolerance_seconds
notes: This atom is intentionally defined at the level of a log analyzer or orchestrator rather than a single HTTP transaction.

It encodes the intuition that if remote and local time deltas agree within a small tolerance (e.g., 5 seconds), then the caller has moderately strong evidence that there was no large hidden gap between the two snapshots.
In BIRCH v0.2 terms, Ra/clock_pair_consistent is one way to justify restart_anchor.anchor_confidence = 'medium' or 'high' without appealing to hidden infrastructure state.

examples.label: Two snapshots ~8 hours apart with matching deltas
examples.lambda: lambda e1, e2: e2['remote_ts'] - e1['remote_ts'] == 28800 and e2['meta']['local_timestamp'] - e1['meta']['local_timestamp'] in (28799, 28800, 28801)
